topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. Tucked beneath a stack of old philosophy texts on the shelf, I found it: a bound manuscript. The title page, written in that elegant, looping script my partner used for poetry, read simply, "The Unseen Subject." My hands trembled as I flipped through the pages. Every chapter, every meticulously detailed scene, was a portrait of my life, my quirks, the mundane moments I hadn't even realized were being cataloged. A cold, heavy feeling settled in my chest, tightening my throat. When he walked in, whistling tunelessly, I didn't look up. I just held the book tighter, my knuckles white. "What is that?" he asked, his tone light, entirely missing the sudden stillness radiating from me. I managed a tight, brittle smile. "Just… old notes. From college."

[story 2]

I found the laptop open on the dining table. The screen glowed with a half-finished chapter, the font stark and professional. It was my life, rendered in prose. My morning coffee, the way I habitually chewed my lip when concentrating, the disastrous attempt at baking sourdough last Tuesday—all documented, analyzed, and woven into a narrative arc. A slow, burning heat started in my jaw. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap wood groaning under my weight. I didn't speak immediately. Instead, I began methodically clearing the crumbs from the table, each movement precise, almost surgical. When he finally noticed my rigid posture, he frowned. "Having trouble with that, darling?" I finally looked up, my eyes flat, devoid of warmth. "No," I said, my voice dangerously level. "I'm just realizing how much detail you manage to capture."

[story 3]

It was the sheer audacity of it that truly unsettled me. I found the journal hidden inside a rarely used drawer, the ink bleeding slightly where my partner had pressed too hard. It wasn't just observation; it was interpretation, a deep dive into the architecture of my personality. Reading the passage describing my irrational fear of pigeons, written with such vivid, almost romantic language, made my stomach churn. I stood by the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape. I didn't want to scream. I didn't want to accuse. Instead, I walked to the kitchen, poured myself a glass of water, and watched the liquid ripple. The silence in the apartment became vast and oppressive, filled only by the internal drumming of my own mounting displeasure. When he approached, I simply took a slow sip, my gaze fixed somewhere beyond him, communicating volumes with the slight tightening around my mouth.
